1: YHWH said to Moses, 'Go in to Pharaoh, for I have hardened his heart and the heart of his servants': For the first time the hardening formula extends beyond Pharaoh to include his court officials (avadim, servants). The Egyptian bureaucracy — the vizier, the priests of the major cults, the royal scribes — formed the cognitive elite of the most literate culture of the ancient world. Their hardening alongside Pharaoh signals that the intellectual capital of Egypt is collectively resistant. The purpose clause that follows — 'that I may show these signs among them' — and the generational pedagogy in v2 establish this phase of the plague cycle explicitly as performative history-making for future teaching.
2: 'That you may tell in the hearing of your son and of your grandson how I have dealt harshly with the Egyptians and what signs I have done among them, so that you may know that I am YHWH': The two generational recipients — bincha (your son) and ben bincha (your son's son) — frame the Passover Haggadah's structure of retelling across generations. The Passover Seder's four questions and narrative responses are rooted precisely in this verse's mandate. The Hebrew verb hit'allalti (I have dealt harshly/made sport) is the Hitpael of alal (to act severely, to show mastery) — the same root that appears in 1 Sam 6:6 when the Philistine priests warn their people not to harden their hearts 'as Egypt and Pharaoh hardened their hearts when God made sport of them.'
3: 'How long will you refuse to humble yourself (anah) before me?': Anah (H6031, to be humbled, to afflict, to be brought low) — the same root used for Israel's affliction in Egypt (Exodus 1:11-12; Genesis 15:13). The question is the moral core of the confrontation: Egypt has afflicted (anah) Israel; now YHWH asks when Pharaoh will allow himself to be humbled (anah) before God. The plague narrative has a recursive poetic justice built into its vocabulary.
4: The locust threat — arbeh (H697, locust): Arbeh derives from rabah (to multiply, to be many) — the locust swarm is named for its multiplicity. Egyptian texts document multiple locust invasions; the Anastasi IV papyrus from the 19th Dynasty describes a locust invasion that destroyed crops. In the ANE, locusts were the most feared sudden agricultural catastrophe because they could denude an entire region in hours. The prophet Joel would later use locusts as the central metaphor for divine judgment (Joel 1:4-7), drawing explicitly on this Exodus prototype.
5: 'They shall cover the face of the earth so that no one can see the earth': The language of covering the earth's surface (vekissa et ein ha'aretz) recalls the flood language (Genesis 7:19-20, the waters covered the face of the earth) and anticipates the Red Sea covering Pharaoh's army (Exodus 14:28). The plague series has a creation-reversal structure: light becomes darkness, abundance becomes desolation, the dry land is overwhelmed. Locust swarms of this density are attested in modern documentation — a large swarm can include 40-80 million insects per square kilometer, making the loss of every edible plant entirely plausible.
6: 'They shall fill your houses, and the houses of all your servants and of all the Egyptians': The plague invades domestic space — the private home was not sanctuary against YHWH's sign. Egyptian houses were carefully constructed to keep out the heat, insects, and Nile flood; the plague violates the domestic boundary that Egyptian architecture was designed to defend. The phrase 'as neither your fathers nor your grandfathers have seen since the day they came on earth to this day' parallels the formula in v2 — the same generational arc, but for catastrophe rather than teaching.
7: Pharaoh's servants said to him, 'How long shall this man be a snare to us?': The officials' rebellion — 'moqesh lanu' (a snare/trap for us) — marks a political fracture in Pharaoh's court that the narrative has been building. The dismissive 'ha'ish hazeh' (this man) uses the demonstrative pronoun in a degrading register — the equivalent of 'this fellow.' By this point Pharaoh's advisors recognize that Moses's God is the determining power, and their pressure on Pharaoh reflects the practical catastrophe: 'Do you not yet understand that Egypt is ruined?'
8: 'Go, serve YHWH your God. But which ones are to go?': Pharaoh's tactical negotiation strategy is to narrow the scope of those leaving. By restricting who may go, he maintains leverage (the women, children, and animals function as hostages) and delegitimizes the exodus as a full national departure. His question 'which ones exactly?' (mi vami haholkhim) is the first concrete attempt at a partial concession that does not resolve the confrontation.
9: 'We will go with our young and our old. We will go with our sons and our daughters and with our flocks and herds': Moses's comprehensive answer — encompassing every demographic and economic category of Israel — is a full social claim: the entire people of God goes. The insistence on taking the flocks and herds is both practical (no sacrifice without animals) and covenantal: the Abrahamic promises cover the whole household, not a male delegation. Moses adds the theological reason: 'for we must hold a feast to YHWH.'
10: 'YHWH be with you, if ever I let you go with your little ones!': Pharaoh's ironic curse — 'May YHWH be with you IF I ever let you go' — is inadvertently prophetic (he will let them go). His accusation that 'evil is in your faces' (ra lefanekhem) may reflect his suspicion that the full family departure means permanent exodus, not a three-day pilgrimage as originally stated. Egyptian administrative texts show that Pharaoh's permission was always narrowly interpreted and frequently retracted.
11: 'Go, the men among you, and serve YHWH, for that is what you are asking': Pharaoh's concession to men only reflects the ancient ANE assumption that religious pilgrimage and military musters were male domains. Allowing adult men to go while retaining women, children, and livestock was a standard ancient hostage-collateral mechanism. The conclusion 'vayegaresh otam me'et penei Par'oh' (they were driven out from Pharaoh's face) ends the negotiation with expulsion, not agreement.
12: YHWH said to Moses, 'Stretch out your hand over the land of Egypt for the locusts': The gesture of Moses stretching his hand (natah yadka) over the land is a consistent command posture throughout the plague narrative. The agency structure is careful: Moses stretches, the east wind (ruach qadim) blows, the locusts come — but the ultimate agent is YHWH who brings the locusts. The east wind (qadim, H6921) is the sirocco, the hot desert wind from the Arabian peninsula; it was associated in Egyptian experience with drying heat and agricultural disaster, and was a known carrier of locust swarms from the eastern desert.
13: Moses stretched out his staff over the land of Egypt, and YHWH brought an east wind upon the land all that day and all that night: The locusts arrive over a 24-hour wind event — not instantaneous magic but a meteorologically coherent catastrophe. When morning came the east wind had brought the locusts. The timing connects to the creation rhythm of evening-and-morning; the plagues participate in the creation week's temporal structure. The locusts, like the hail, come on a predictable schedule that YHWH controls and Moses can announce in advance — the predictive precision itself being one of the most disorienting features for Egyptian observers.
14: The locusts came up over all the land of Egypt and settled on the whole country of Egypt, such a dense swarm of locusts as had never been before, nor ever will be again: The superlative formula 'never been before nor ever will be again' (lifanav lo haya khen arbeh kamohu ve'acharav lo yihyeh ken) is used for all three final plague events — the hail (Exodus 9:18,24), the locusts (Exodus 10:14), and the darkness (Exodus 10:21,23), and the cry of anguish at the death of the firstborn (Exodus 11:6). Each plague in the final triad is uniquely unparalleled; the escalation culminates in the death of the firstborn.
15: They covered the face of the whole land, so that the land was darkened: The pre-darkness darkness of the locust swarm — vatekhash ha'aretz (the land was darkened) — foreshadows the ninth plague. The darkening of a locust swarm is historically attested: Josephus describes locust swarms blocking out the sun (Ant. 2.305). The destruction is total: 'not a green thing remained, neither tree nor plant of the field, through all the land of Egypt.' After the hail destroyed the standing grain and fruit trees (Exodus 9:25), the locusts devour whatever re-sprouted — a double agricultural catastrophe that would take multiple growing seasons to recover from.
16: Then Pharaoh hurriedly called Moses and Aaron and said, 'I have sinned against YHWH your God, and against you': The word maher (quickly, hurriedly) — Pharaoh summons Moses with urgency, not dignity. His confession in v16 is the most syntactically complete admission of guilt in the plague narrative: 'I have sinned (chatati) against YHWH your God and against you.' The double object — against God and against Moses — is unusual; it recognizes both vertical (covenant-breaking) and horizontal (political injustice) dimensions of his sin. The same double-direction confession appears in Psalms 51:4.
17: 'Now therefore, forgive my sin, please, only this once, and plead with YHWH your God only to remove this death from me': The phrase 'only this once' (rak hapam) implies he knows this pattern of confession-relief-re-hardening may recur; he is negotiating each crisis episodically. The characterization of the locusts as 'this death' (hammavet hazeh) reflects the agricultural reality: crop destruction was not merely inconvenience but a food-security catastrophe that would cause mass death through famine — an outcome Egypt remembered from the Famine Stela and from Genesis 41's seven-year famine context.
18: So Moses went out from Pharaoh and pleaded with YHWH: Moses interceding immediately and effectively signals his standing before YHWH — the Pharaoh must use Moses as his intercessor with the God he refuses to obey. The structural irony is complete: Egypt's gods cannot help Pharaoh; he must borrow the prayers of the man he oppressed.
19: YHWH turned the wind into a very strong west wind (ruach yam), which lifted the locusts and drove them into the Red Sea (yam suph): Ruach yam (literally 'sea wind') is the Mediterranean wind from the northwest — the prevailing wind of Egypt's summer and the natural counter to the sirocco. Yam suph (H3220+H5488) — the sea of reeds/rushes — is the same body of water that will swallow Pharaoh's army; locusts precede the chariots into its depths. 'Not a single locust was left in all the country of Egypt' contrasts with what the locusts left: not a plant remained (v15). The completeness in both directions confirms YHWH's total sovereignty over the reversal.
20: But YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he did not let the people of Israel go: The post-locust hardening is the seventh in the series. The plague-relief-hardening cycle has its own rhythm by Exodus 10; the reader recognizes the pattern. Theologically, each re-hardening demonstrates that mercy — the removal of judgment — does not inherently produce repentance; only the knowledge of God (the recognition formula) is the stated goal.
21: Then YHWH said to Moses, 'Stretch out your hand toward heaven, that there may be darkness over the land of Egypt, a darkness to be felt (veyamesh choshekh)': The darkness (choshekh, H2822) is emphatically tactile: yemashesh (can be felt, groped, touched) from mashash (H4959, to feel/grope in the dark). The darkness is not merely the absence of light but a palpable substance. Egyptian religious culture was organized around the daily solar cycle — Ra's journey through the sky and the underworld was the cosmic frame for all time; Egyptian temples were oriented to the sunrise; the pharaoh's daily ritual involved welcoming Ra at dawn. The ninth plague attacks the entire Egyptian cosmological and liturgical order by extinguishing the sun.
22: So Moses stretched out his hand toward heaven, and there was pitch darkness (choshekh afelah) in all the land of Egypt three days: Choshekh afelah (H2822+H653, thick/dark darkness) — the combination of synonyms intensifies the darkness beyond natural night into something supernatural. Afelah appears in Deuteronomy 28:29 as a curse ('you shall grope at noonday as a blind person gropes in darkness') and in Isaiah 8:22 and 59:9 as an image of judgment. Three days of darkness in Egypt echoes the three days proposed by Moses for the pilgrimage (Exodus 3:18; Exodus 5:3; Exodus 8:27) — the same duration Pharaoh refused becomes the duration of the judgment.
23: They did not see one another. No one rose from his place for three days, but all the people of Israel had light where they lived: The spatial separation of light and darkness — light for Goshen, darkness for Egypt — is the most dramatic application of the plague-discrimination principle begun with the fly plague (Exodus 8:23). Egyptian administrative texts and burial literature depict the terror of darkness; the inability to navigate even inside one's house for three full days would be psychologically and logistically catastrophic. All commerce, agriculture, cultic rituals, and court administration stopped. The contrast 'but all the people of Israel had light in their dwellings' (ulkhol benei Yisrael hayah or bemoshvotam) is the light-darkness binary of creation (Genesis 1:3-4) applied to covenant distinction.
24: Then Pharaoh called Moses and said, 'Go, serve YHWH; your little ones also may go with you; only let your flocks and your herds remain behind': Pharaoh's penultimate concession — now including children — but retaining the livestock as economic collateral. This is the most generous concession yet, almost complete; the livestock are the last negotiating chip. The progression of concessions across the plague cycle (men only → men and women → men, women, and children → men, women, children, and flocks) mirrors a classic treaty-negotiation pattern in which each round of pressure extracts one more concession.
25: But Moses said, 'You must also let us have sacrifices and burnt offerings, that we may sacrifice to YHWH our God. Our livestock also must go with us': Moses's argument is liturgically coherent: no Passover, no dedication offerings, no sacrificial system without animals. 'We do not know what we must use to serve YHWH until we arrive there' — the openness about the specific form of worship establishes genuine cultic intent, not mere pretext for escape.
26: 'Not a hoof shall be left behind': The absolute formulation 'lo tisha'er parsa' (not a hoof shall be left) closes the negotiation permanently. Moses's intransigence on this point mirrors YHWH's intransigence on Israel's complete departure; the divine claim on all of Israel — body, family, and property — admits no partial accommodation. The totality of liberation is as important as liberation itself.
27: But YHWH hardened Pharaoh's heart, and he would not let them go: The eighth hardening in the plague narrative. By this point, every genuine concession Pharaoh made has been reversed; the pattern establishes that Pharaoh's intermittent agreements are never sincere, and YHWH's reinforcement of his resistance ensures that the confrontation will be fully resolved only by the tenth plague and the sea crossing — not by negotiated compromise.
28: Then Pharaoh said to Moses, 'Get away from me; take care never to see my face again, for on the day you see my face you shall die': The royal threat of death for an unsummoned audience was standard in ancient Near Eastern courts (cf. Esther 4:11 — death for approaching the Persian king without being summoned). Pharaoh's declaration is simultaneously a royal sentence and an unconscious prophecy: the day Pharaoh next 'sees Moses' effectively (when Moses announces the final plague in Exodus 11) is the day that leads to Pharaoh's destruction — the reversal of the death threat upon himself.
29: Moses said, 'As you say! I will not see your face again': Moses's agreement — 'ken dibarta' (you have spoken rightly/as you say) — closes the formal diplomatic relationship between Moses and Pharaoh. The statement anticipates that the next encounter (Exodus 11, the announcement of the death of the firstborn) is Moses's final word to Pharaoh before the exodus. The break in direct address is structural: the plague and exodus narrative now moves forward without further negotiation. YHWH has been present in the deliberation from the beginning; Pharaoh's court has expelled God's spokesman.
